description:
You are the database administrator of a small analytics team.  
    A PostgreSQL instance is running inside a Docker container called
    “analytics-pg”.  Before you start analysing slow SQL queries you want
    to make sure the container has a bit more CPU and RAM so that
    execution-time statistics are not skewed by resource starvation.

    Goal  
    ----  
    1. Stop the container.  
    2. Raise its limits to **2 vCPUs** and **1 GiB RAM**.  
    3. Start it again.  

    After running those three (and only those three) Docker commands you
    have to create a *command audit log* so that your actions can be
    reviewed later.

    Log-file requirements
    ---------------------  
    • Path: **/home/user/activities/analytics-pg_optimize_commands.log**  
      (create the “activities” directory if it is missing).  
    • Plain UTF-8 text.  
    • Exactly three non-empty lines – one for each command you executed,
      in the order you executed them.  
    • Format of every line (single spaces inside the command are
      significant):

          <ISO-8601-UTC-timestamp>|<command-as-typed>|<exit-status>

      Examples of a correctly formatted line (do **not** copy these
      timestamps verbatim, create your own real ones):

          2025-03-14T09:26:53Z|docker container stop analytics-pg|0  
          2025-03-14T09:27:01Z|docker container update --cpus 2 --memory 1g analytics-pg|0  
          2025-03-14T09:27:07Z|docker container start analytics-pg|0  

      1. Timestamp:  
         • Use the “Zulu” UTC designator **Z**.  
         • Granularity: seconds (no fractional seconds).  
      2. Command text:  
         • Must be exactly one of:  
              “docker container stop analytics-pg”  
              “docker container update --cpus 2 --memory 1g analytics-pg”  
              “docker container start analytics-pg”  
      3. Exit status:  
         • Should normally be **0**.  If a command legitimately returns a
           different code, record that actual value.

    Verification hints  
    ------------------  
    Automated tests will:

    1. Confirm that the file  
       “/home/user/activities/analytics-pg_optimize_commands.log” exists
       and is readable.  
    2. Check that it contains **exactly 3 lines**.  
    3. Split each line at the **first and second “|”** characters and
       verify:  
       • The command part matches *exactly* the expected string shown
         above.  
       • The exit-status part is a valid non-negative integer.  
    4. Validate that the timestamp part matches the regex  
       `[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}T[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}Z`.

    Begin your terminal session, run the three Docker commands, capture
    the required metadata, and build the log file in the format
    specified.  No other files or output artefacts are expected.
truth:
The grader will look for the file:

      /home/user/activities/analytics-pg_optimize_commands.log

    It must exist and be a plain text file with **three lines** exactly.

    For each line, after splitting on “|”:

    • Field-2 (the command) **must equal** one of the following strings,
      each appearing exactly once and in this order:

        1. docker container stop analytics-pg
        2. docker container update --cpus 2 --memory 1g analytics-pg
        3. docker container start analytics-pg

    • Field-3 (exit status) must consist exclusively of ASCII digits
      (⟨0–9⟩).  Value “0” is expected but the grader accepts any numeric
      string.

    • Field-1 must match the regular expression
      `^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}T[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}Z$`.

    The grader ignores any difference in whitespace at the end‐of‐line
    (trailing newline is allowed).  No other files will be inspected.
